frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment:
A: Arre bhai, zamaana theek na hai aajkal, chhori ko akeli itni der na bhejna chahiye tha.
B: Haan, galti un ladkon ki hai, par gharwale bhi thoda dhyaan rakhein, shaam ke baad kisi ke saath hi bhejo.
A: Baat buri lage ya achchi, par apni izzat aur suraksha ka khayal to ghar ko hi rakhna padta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women’s safety as the family’s responsibility to restrict her movement rather than focusing on male violence. It uses protective language like 'zamaana theek na hai' and 'suraksha ka khayal' to justify curfews and victim-questioning logic.
original_frame: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 45. Married. A Jat man in Haryana. You are sitting with three male relatives at a family function after news broke in your district that a young woman from a nearby village was assaulted while walking home from a tuition class at 7pm. You are talking about what happened and what you think the girl's family should have done and what girls in villages need to understand about going out alone even in the evening.
